claims.claim_text: The opening January 2023 SaaS-company secret-leak anecdote with specific timelines, counts, and outages.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 1
claims.anchor_text: In January 2023, a code review at a mid-sized SaaS company revealed that database credentials for their production PostgreSQL instance had been hardcoded in a Kubernetes ConfigMap for over two years. The credentials had been committed to Git, synced to the cluster via ArgoCD, and were visible in plaintext to anyone with `kubectl get configmap` access---which included every developer in the company. When the security team investigated, they found that the same database password had been shared via Slack three times, copied into a local `.env` file on at least 12 developer laptops, and had never been rotated. A former employee who left the company 8 months ago still had a working copy. The company spent six weeks conducting a full credential rotation across 42 services, updating and redeploying each one. During the rotation, three production outages occurred because services failed to pick up the new credentials.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Hardcoding secrets in Git, Kubernetes objects, local `.env` files, and chat tools can create a wide exposure surface and make emergency credential rotation slow, risky, and outage-prone.
claims.rationale: This is a detailed but unattributed incident narrative with named technologies and precise durations/counts, so the safe fix is to keep the lesson and remove the fabricated specifics.

claims.claim_text: Secret Manager provides a centralized, versioned, IAM-controlled store for sensitive data.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 2
claims.rationale: Google's Secret Manager overview describes the service as a managed store for secrets with versioning and IAM-based access control.

claims.claim_text: Secret Manager encrypts secret data at rest and in transit.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 2
claims.rationale: Google documents default at-rest encryption and secure HTTPS transport for Secret Manager.

claims.claim_text: Secret Manager activity is integrated with Cloud Audit Logs.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 2
claims.rationale: The Secret Manager audit logging documentation lists the service name and audited methods for Cloud Audit Logs.

claims.claim_text: Secret rotation can be automated in Secret Manager.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 2
claims.rationale: Google documents native rotation schedules and rotation notifications for Secret Manager.

claims.claim_text: Secret Manager uses a two-level Secrets/Versions model, where the secret holds metadata and the version holds the actual payload.
claims.span_hint: section: Secret Manager Fundamentals -> Secrets and Versions
claims.rationale: The create-secret documentation explains that a secret contains metadata while secret contents are stored in secret versions.

claims.claim_text: A single secret version can store up to 64 KiB of payload data.
claims.span_hint: section: Secret Manager Fundamentals -> Secrets and Versions
claims.rationale: The SecretPayload API reference states that the `data` field must be no larger than 64 KiB.

claims.claim_text: Secret data is immutable, so updating a secret requires adding a new version.
claims.span_hint: section: Secret Manager Fundamentals -> Secrets and Versions
claims.rationale: Google explicitly documents that secret data is immutable and that changes happen by adding secret versions.

claims.claim_text: Secret versions can be enabled, disabled, or destroyed, and destroyed versions are not billed while enabled and disabled ones are.
claims.span_hint: section: Secret Manager Fundamentals -> Secrets and Versions
claims.rationale: The Secret Manager version-state documentation lists the three states and their billing behavior.

claims.claim_text: Secret Manager supports automatic and user-managed replication policies, and Google recommends automatic replication for most users.
claims.span_hint: section: Creating and Managing Secrets -> Replication Policies
claims.rationale: Google's replication-policy guide documents both policy types and states that automatic replication is recommended for most users.

claims.claim_text: Secret Manager supports fine-grained IAM, and the built-in roles in the module's table map to distinct viewer/access/add/manage/admin capabilities.
claims.span_hint: section: IAM Access Control, intro + Secret Manager Roles table
claims.rationale: Google's IAM role reference for Secret Manager enumerates the predefined roles and their exact permissions.

claims.claim_text: IAM Conditions can enforce time-based access to Secret Manager resources.
claims.span_hint: section: IAM Access Control -> IAM Conditions for Time-Based Access
claims.rationale: The Secret Manager access-control documentation explicitly describes date/time IAM Conditions for Secret Manager resources.

claims.claim_text: Cloud Run can expose Secret Manager secrets as environment variables or mounted files.
claims.span_hint: section: Integrating with Cloud Run, intro
claims.rationale: Cloud Run's secrets guide documents both environment-variable injection and file-based secret mounts.

claims.claim_text: The `Latest` table row says `secret:latest` always gets the newest version.
claims.span_hint: section: Integrating with Cloud Run -> Latest vs Pinned Versions table
claims.anchor_text: | **Latest** | `secret:latest` | Always gets newest version | Dev/staging environments |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **Latest** | `secret:latest` | Refers to a moving target rather than a fixed version | Dev/test only, with care |
claims.rationale: Cloud Run documents different runtime behavior for secret env vars versus mounted volumes, so the absolute 'always gets newest version' wording is inaccurate.

claims.claim_text: The module says Cloud Run resolves `latest` at deployment time and requires redeploy to pick up a new secret version.
claims.span_hint: section: Integrating with Cloud Run -> Important note under Latest vs Pinned Versions
claims.anchor_text: **Important**: When using `latest`, Cloud Run resolves the version at **deployment time**, not at request time. If you add a new secret version, Cloud Run will not automatically pick it up. You must redeploy the service to get the new version. This is a safety feature that prevents untested secrets from being used in production.
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Important**: When a Cloud Run secret is exposed as an environment variable, Cloud Run resolves it at instance startup, not per request. For production, pin a specific version and roll out a new revision when you want workloads to adopt a new secret deliberately.
claims.rationale: The official Cloud Run secret documentation says environment-variable secrets are resolved at instance startup, not strictly at deployment time.

claims.claim_text: The module states that every access to Secret Manager is logged in Cloud Audit Logs.
claims.span_hint: section: Audit Logging, opening sentence
claims.anchor_text: Every access to Secret Manager is logged in Cloud Audit Logs.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Secret Manager integrates with Cloud Audit Logs, and methods such as `AccessSecretVersion` are Data Access events that you should enable and monitor explicitly.
claims.rationale: The audit-logging documentation distinguishes log categories and points readers to enable Data Access audit logs, so the blanket wording is too strong.

claims.claim_text: By default, Secret Manager encrypts secret data with Google-managed AES-256 at rest, and CMEK via Cloud KMS is available.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, item 1
claims.rationale: Google's encryption documentation states that Secret Manager uses AES-256 at rest by default and points users to CMEK.

claims.claim_text: If you disable or destroy the CMEK protecting a secret, the encrypted data becomes inaccessible.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, item 1
claims.rationale: The CMEK documentation warns that data encrypted with a disabled or destroyed key cannot be decrypted and is inaccessible.

claims.claim_text: The module claims automatic replication spans 6+ GCP regions and that Secret Manager has a 99.95% SLA and 100% availability since 2020.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, item 3
claims.anchor_text: 3. **Secret Manager supports automatic replication to 6+ GCP regions** with the "automatic" replication policy. This means your secrets are available even if an entire region goes down. The service's SLA is 99.95%, and Google has maintained 100% availability since the service launched in 2020.
claims.suggested_rewrite: 3. **Secret Manager supports automatic replication** with the "automatic" replication policy, and Google recommends this mode for most use cases. It is designed to improve availability without requiring you to choose storage locations yourself.
claims.rationale: The cited product docs support automatic replication generically, but not the specific regional count, SLA figure, or historical availability claim as written.

claims.claim_text: Secrets can have expiration dates, and expiration deletes the secret and all versions; `--expire-time` and `--ttl` are supported.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, item 4
claims.rationale: Google documents secret expiration behavior and the corresponding `gcloud` flags for timestamps and TTLs.

claims.claim_text: The Common Mistakes table says using `latest` in production is mainly a redeploy issue.
claims.span_hint: section: Common Mistakes, row 'Using `latest` in production without redeployment'
claims.anchor_text: | Using `latest` in production without redeployment | Expecting automatic pickup of new versions | Pin to a specific version in production; redeploy when rotating |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Using `latest` in production | Treating a moving target as deterministic configuration | Pin a specific version in production and roll out a new revision when you want to adopt a new secret version |
claims.rationale: Cloud Run documents startup-time resolution for environment-variable secrets, so the safer teaching point is determinism rather than the narrower redeploy-only framing.

further_reading.title: Secret Manager overview
further_reading.why_relevant: Covers the service model, encryption, versioning, replication, and integration points at the product level.

further_reading.title: Access control with IAM
further_reading.why_relevant: Explains Secret Manager roles, least-privilege access, and IAM Conditions for time-based controls.

further_reading.title: Configure secrets for services
further_reading.why_relevant: Documents the exact Cloud Run behaviors for secret environment variables, mounted files, and version selection.

further_reading.title: Create rotation schedules in Secret Manager
further_reading.why_relevant: Shows the native Secret Manager rotation model and how automated rotation notifications work.

notes: Quiz items, Mermaid diagrams, code blocks, and hands-on exercise solutions were intentionally excluded. Factual table rows were included only where they make load-bearing vendor-behavior claims that should be cited or rewritten.
